question: [File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: filterMessage(TopicMessage message, TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of a sequenceNumber check in filterMessage() to receive duplicate fee-related messages (e.g., if the same message is re-delivered by the underlying listener due to a retry), causing the client to process the same fee parameter change multiple times and potentially act on stale or repeated fee data?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user set startTime to the maximum allowed value just below DomainUtils.now() and endTime to startTime+1 (minimum valid range per isValidEndTime()), creating a near-zero time window subscription that repeatedly polls the database for fee messages in a tight loop, causing excessive database load?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getTopicListener()] Can an unprivileged external user, when the system is configured with SHARED_POLL listener type, exploit the fact that SharedPollingTopicListener uses a single shared Flux for all subscribers (getSharedListener returns topicMessages regardless of filter), causing their subscription to receive fee-related messages from ALL topics (not just their requested topicId), with only the downstream filterMessage() as the sole guard?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lazy initialization of the consensusLatencyTimer (via @Getter(lazy=true)) by triggering concurrent first-access from multiple threads, causing a race condition in the lazy getter initialization that results in multiple Timer instances being registered with the MeterRegistry, corrupting fee-related latency metrics?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: filterMessage(TopicMessage message, TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() does not validate message.getTopicId() for null, such that if a malformed TopicMessage with a null topicId is injected into the stream, the equals() call throws a NullPointerException that terminates the Flux and disrupts fee message delivery for all subscribers on that stream?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user subscribe to a fee-schedule topic with a startTime far in the past, causing the PollingTopicListener to retrieve maxPageSize (up to 5000) fee messages per poll interval, and by maintaining this subscription indefinitely, continuously drain database resources at the maximum page rate outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: recordMetric(TopicMessage topicMessage)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a fee-related topic message with consensusTimestamp set to exactly Long.MAX_VALUE, causing the division Long.MAX_VALUE / 1000000 to produce a value larger than System.currentTimeMillis(), resulting in a negative latency that is silently dropped, creating a permanent blind spot in fee message latency monitoring?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the Flux pipeline applies filter(t -> filterMessage(t, filter)) after the underlying listener emits messages, meaning the underlying listener (especially SharedPollingTopicListener) processes and emits ALL topic messages to ALL subscribers before filtering, causing unnecessary CPU and memory usage for fee-unrelated message processing?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: filterMessage(TopicMessage message, TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the consensusTimestamp >= startTime comparison where startTime is a nanosecond-precision long, by setting startTime to 0 (valid per @Min(0)), to receive every fee-related message ever recorded in the mirror node database, enabling complete reconstruction of the fee schedule modification history?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of connection-level authentication in the gRPC listen() endpoint to subscribe to fee-schedule topics without any identity verification, receiving all fee parameter updates in real time and using this information to front-run fee changes in their transaction submissions?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: getTopicListener()] Can an unprivileged external user, by observing the error message 'Unknown listener type: ' + type thrown in the default case of getTopicListener(), infer the exact ListenerType enum value that caused the failure, leaking internal configuration state that reveals which listener backend is active and enabling targeted attacks against that specific backend's fee message handling?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the reactive backpressure model of the Flux pipeline by deliberately consuming messages slowly (not requesting elements), causing the upstream listener to buffer fee-related messages in memory up to maxBufferSize (16384 default), and by opening many such slow subscriptions, exhaust JVM heap memory and crash the service?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: filterMessage(TopicMessage message, TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() has no check on message.getPayerAccountId(), allowing them to receive fee-related messages paid by any account including system accounts, leaking the payerAccountId of fee schedule update transactions and revealing which accounts are authorized to modify fees?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that listen() does not validate the topicId in the filter against a whitelist of permitted topics, allowing them to subscribe to any topic including system-reserved fee-schedule topics (e.g., 0.0.111, 0.0.112), receiving fee parameter data that should be restricted to authorized consumers?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: recordMetric(TopicMessage topicMessage)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Timer.record() call in recordMetric() by injecting fee-related messages with carefully crafted consensusTimestamp values that produce latency values at histogram bucket boundaries, causing the Micrometer timer histogram to allocate excessive memory for bucket tracking and degrade monitoring system performance?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the REDIS listener's channel naming convention (topic.{topicId}) by subscribing to a fee-schedule topic, and if the Redis channel is also writable by other services, inject crafted fee-related messages into the Redis channel that bypass the consensus validation and appear as legitimate fee updates to all subscribers?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: filterMessage(TopicMessage message, TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a runningHash validation in filterMessage() to receive fee-related messages whose runningHash has been tampered with in the database, causing the client to accept fee parameter data with broken integrity guarantees outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the Flux returned by listen() applies doOnNext(this::recordMetric) for every message, and by subscribing to a high-volume fee-related topic, cause the recordMetric() method to be called millions of times per second, creating a CPU bottleneck in the metric recording path that degrades fee message throughput for all users?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user set limit to Long.MAX_VALUE in TopicMessageFilter (satisfying @Min(0)), causing hasLimit() to return true but the limit-based page size calculation in PollingTopicListener to overflow when computing (int)(filter.getLimit() - context.getCount().get()), resulting in a negative page size that causes unexpected behavior in fee message retrieval?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: filterMessage(TopicMessage message, TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that filterMessage() does not check message.getChunkNum() or message.getChunkTotal(), allowing them to receive partial chunks of a multi-part fee schedule update message and reconstruct the full fee parameter payload from individually filtered chunks, bypassing any chunk-level access controls?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/listener/CompositeTopicListener.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: listen(TopicMessageFilter filter)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of subscription count limits per client IP or identity in listen() to open thousands of parallel subscriptions to fee-schedule topics, each independently polling the database, causing a thundering herd effect that overwhelms the database connection pool and prevents legitimate fee update processing?
